text:
Admission Date:  1997-04-04              Discharge Date:   1990-02-13

Date of Birth:  2000-03-23             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:George
Chief Complaint:
SVC Syndrome

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
06-02 tracheal bronchial stent placement
03-27 Svc stent placement: 6mm and 8mm Stent placed


History of Present Illness:
57 year old male from [**State 1727**] with CAD s/p MI x2 (1973, 2017),
?COPD (on 2L home O2, 3ppd smoking for years), atrial flutter on
coumadin, found to have new lung mass with recent onset of SVC
syndrome, s/p bx at VA 99938 Galvan Tunnel
South Kevin, NJ 25001-6849, now transferred to Baycare Alliant Hospital for
airway protection prior to initiation of XRT.
.
Patient initially went to Lovelace Rehabilitation Hospital Center in
1988-08-08 for CP. W/u revealed a new mediastinal mass and he
was transferred to Togus VA for further workup. An initial
bronchoscopy was performed but biopsies have been nondiagnostic.
A f/u CT on 1997-01-12 revealed spread of the mass to the
right lung. He also developed pneumonia during this time and
went into new-onset atrial flutter during another bronchoscopy
which had to be aborted because of this event. He was started on
diltiazem and coumadin. However, his rate was difficult to
control. He underwent repeat bronchoscopy in 05 but obtained
tissue only revealed necrosis per the patient.
.
The patient was eventually transferred to the VA 99938 Galvan Tunnel
South Kevin, NJ 25001-6849 for
CT-guided biopsy given these unsuccessful attempts to make a
diagnosis. He was initially on the floor after admission on
1979-06-11 but transferred to the CCU b/o 6-8 seconds pauses after
diltiazem, digoxin and atenolol given for treatment resistent
atrial flutter with RVR in the 150s. His coumadin was held for
the biopsy and he was kept on a heparin drip. His rate remained
difficult to be controlled despite several treatment attempts.
He became symptomatic after relative bradycardia to the 60s
(nausea, dizziness, lightheadedness and diarrhea). His symptoms
abated after CBB reversal and dopamine drip.
.
His course was c/b wheezing and SOB as well as increasing SVC
syndrome with swelling of his face, both arms and ears. He
initially received a 5-day course of PO prednisone for wheezing
but was switched to IV decadrone to reduce swelling from his SVC
compression. He was also started on azithromycin for
post-obstructive PNA.
.
The CT-guided biopsy showed sarcoma versus melanoma. Plans were
made to provide XRT at 9042 Cortez Meadow
Port Heather, MS 15977 VA. However, he was
transferred eventually to Baycare Alliant Hospital because of the concern for
airway compromise during XRT. He was transferred on Verapamil
and Digoxin for rate control as well as a heparin drip.
.
On ROS, the patient denies any fevers but nightsweats since
1999, weightloss (10 lbs in one month) with decreased appetite,
and cough with clear sputum and occasional brownish clots coming
up. He denies frank hemoptysis, N/V or SOB in rest. However, he
did become more SOB over the last few months, most recently he
can only walk to the bathroom w/o dyspnea. He is on 2L home O2.
He denies any CP, constipation, urinary symptoms or blood in the
stool (although he does have bleeding hemorrhoids with
occasional blood on top of the stool).


Past Medical History:
Hyperlipidemia
CAD, s/p MI (1973 + 2017), each time s/p Matthew Jackson
PTSD
Chronic LBP
Depression
Atrial flutter, on coumadin (as above)
?COPD


Social History:
150 pack year h/o smoking (quit 4 months ago b/o increasing
dyspnea), occasional alcohol in the past, Hart 96521 Mendoza Falls
South Juanshire, TN 48295 exposure
in the past. Lives with partner in Joshua Fuller. Has wheelchair to get
around.


Family History:
No malignancy. Strong FHx of diabetes on mother's side. No heart
disease that he knows of.


Physical Exam:
VS: Temp: 96.6 BP: 108/72 HR: 156 RR: 14 O2sat 98% on 5L NC
GEN: pleasant, comfortable, NAD
HEENT: PERRL, EOMI, anicteric, bluish discoloration of face and
ears, MMM, op without lesions, uvula swollen
NECK: severely swollen with elevated neck veins
RESP: diffuse wheezes throughout, no rhales or rhonchi
CV: tachycardic, S1 and S2 wnl, no m/r/g
ABD: nd, +b/s, soft, nt, no masses felt
EXT: 05-01+ LE edema b/l (chronic), 2+ b/l UE swelling, warm feet,
good pulses
SKIN: bluish discoloration of face and ears as above/no jaundice

NEURO: AAOx3. Cn II-XII intact. 5/5 strength throughout.


Pertinent Results:
AM Labs from 1997-04-04 at VA 99938 Galvan Tunnel
South Kevin, NJ 25001-6849:
Na 140, K 4.8, BUN/Cr 22/0.8
WBC 17.7, Hct 29.1, Plt 163
PT 12.1, PTT 64.2, INR 1.0
.
CT on 1989-09-26 at VA 99938 Galvan Tunnel
South Kevin, NJ 25001-6849: SVC attenuated inferiorly.
Persistent R paratracheal, suprahilar mass with post-obstructive
PNA. Consolidation of RUL. Wide-spread metastatic disease,
mediastinal and hilar LAD. Pericardial LAD. B/l supraclav. LAD.
.
TTE on 2016-03-23 at VA: EF 50%. Encroachment of lung mass on
pericardium.
.
.
Baycare Alliant Hospital STUDIES:
CHEST (PORTABLE AP)  1997-04-04 7:23 PM: Opacification of the
right upper lung likely represents lung collapse due to proximal
obstruction. It is associated with moderate sized right pleural
effusion and compression of the lower trachea. Multiple masses
in the left lung field measure up to 3.7 cm. Cardiac size is
normal with prominence of the left atrial appendage.
.
CHEST (PORTABLE AP)  1984-07-14 7:20 PM: There has been no
appreciable radiographic change. Right upper lobe is collapsed
around a mass and multiple nodules are seen in the remaining
portion of the right lung and left. There is probably a small
left pleural effusion. The stent is not radiographically
apparent. There is no pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum
.
CHEST (PORTABLE AP)  2008-12-18 10:33 AM
No change, given slight differences in technique, to the
opacified right hemithorax reflective of effusion and right
upper lobe collapse.
.
00481466-e60d-9f5b-779d-d75783684875 EXTREM BILAT VENOGRAPHY  2002-01-31 8:54 AM
Reason: stent placement in SVC
Contrast: OPTIRAY
PROCEDURE AND FINDINGS: Informed consent was obtained from the
patient explaining the risks and benefits of the procedure. The
patient was placed supine on the angiographic table and the
right arm was prepped and draped in standard sterile fashion.
Using ultrasonographic guidance and a micropuncture sheath
system, access was gained into the right basilic vein and a
0.018 guidewire and 5 Fr sheath was placed. The inner dilator
was then removed as well as the wire and a 0.035 Thomas wire
was placed up to the level of the subclavian vein under
fluoroscopic guidance. The vascular sheath was exchanged for a
5- French Young Tip sheath and a right subclavian venogram was
then performed. The venogram demonstrated no antegrade
opacification/flow through the SVC, with retrograde flow in the
right IJ and collaterals. Using a 0.035 Thomas wire, the 5-
French Young Tip sheath was exchanged for a 7- French Young Tip
sheath. Using a combination of a 5- French Kumpe catheter and a
0.035 Glidewire, access was gained into the SVC and then
distally into the inferior vena cava under fluoroscopic
guidance. The Kumpe catheter was advanced all the way into the
inferior vena cava and the Glidewire was exchanged for a 0.035
Green wire that was placed with tip in the inferior vena cava
preserving access. The 5- French Kumpe catheter was removed and
then reinserted through the 7 Fr sheath alongside the Green
wire. The Kumpe and Glidewire were then used to selectively
catheterize the left brachiocephalic vein, and a left
brachiocephalic venogram performed. This showed mixed retrograde
and antegrade flow, with mild narrowing of the left
brachiocephalic vein at its confluence with the right.The Kumpe
catheter and a Bentson guidewire were then negotiated through
the severely narrowed SVC into the right atrium. Pull-back
SVC-grams were performed delineating the anatomy and extent of
the severe extrinsic SVC narrowing/encasement. Based on the
diagnostic findings, it was decided to perform stenting.
A 10 mm x 4 cm PTA balloon was then advanced over the wire in
order to partially dilate the SVC prior to stent placement. A 14
mm x 6 cm SMART stent was placed into the SVC under fluoroscopic
guidance, with its lower end at the SVC/RA junction and its
upper end just below the left and right brachiocephalic
confluence. PTA of the stent was then performed with a 12 mm
balloon.
A followup venogram demonstrated narrowing with persistent
retrograde flow in the right brachiocephalic, above the stent.
A 14mm x 8 cm SMART stent was then placed in the right
brachiocephalic vein, overlapping with the SVC stent. This was
followed by balloon dilation of the stent with a 12 mm balloon.
A 5-Fr straight Omniflush catheter was then placed with tip
within the proximal upper stent. Venogram demonstrated good
angiographic results with brisk antegrade flow through the
stents into the right atrium.
The 7- French Young Tip sheath was exchanged over a guidewire
for a 7 Fr x 20cm triple lumen line, placed with tip in the
right axillary vein. This was placed to function as a midline,
in lieu of the requested PICC line, with better hemostasis at
the basilic vein entry site. The three ports were flushed and
the catheter was sutured to the skin with 0 silk sutures.
IMPRESSION: Severe extrinsic narrowing/encasement of the SVC,
with moderate extrinsic narrowing of the right, and mild of the
left, brachiocephalic veins.
Successful placement of two 14 mm SMART stents in the superior
vena cava and right brachiocephalic vein, with good angiographic
results on the final venogram.
Midline venous access with placement of right basilic triple
lumen catheter with tip in the right axillary vein.
.
FNA, Lung, Right Middle Lobe:
  (Consult slides, PSC 1090, Box 0055
APO AA 28865-3179 Schoolcraft Memorial Hospital #: C07-4900):

  POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS.

  Malignant spindle cell tumor (see note).

  Note: The differential diagnosis includes metastatic sarcoma
and
  primary sarcomatoid carcinoma. Review of immunohistochemical
studies
  performed at PSC 1090, Box 0055
APO AA 28865-3179 Schoolcraft Memorial Hospital shows positive staining of the
tumor
  cells with vimentin and CD68 and negative staining with
desmin, actin,
  smooth muscle actin, S-100, HMB-45, pankeratin and CD34.
  Per Dr. Kim. Brahma at the Schoolcraft Memorial Hospital, additional stains show positive

  staining with p53 and CD10, and negative staining with CD30
and TTF-1.
  Further immunohistochemical studies were performed at Alliancehealth Madill
  (Baycare Alliant Hospital) showing negative staining of the tumor cells with p63
and MNF116
  and few scattered cells showing positive staining with CK903,
favored to
  be normal epithelial cells within the tumor. The
immunohistochemical
  findings are non-specific.
  Dr. Jason Parker has reviewed the slides and concurs with
the
  diagnosis.
.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient is a 57 yo old male from [**State 1727**] with CAD s/p MI x2
(1973, 2017), ?COPD (on 2L home O2, 3ppd smoking for years),
atrial flutter on coumadin, found to have new lung mass with
recent onset of SVC syndrome, s/p bx at VA 99938 Galvan Tunnel
South Kevin, NJ 25001-6849, transferred
to Baycare Alliant Hospital for airway protection prior to initiation of XRT, now
s/p bronch and y-stent placement by IP and SVC stent by IR. Due
to poor prognosis and lack of clinical improvement he was made
comfort measures only, underwent 9 cycles of palliative XRT.
Hospital course by problem:
.
# Increased dyspnea/wheezing: Patient was not aware of a
diagnosis of COPD but had been on nebulizer treatments for
several years, also on home oxygen. Had also recently been
started on formoterol and momKayla Walker. Increased wheezing was
thought secondary to COPD as well as large lung mass. Pt
presented with dypsnea and increased WOB that initially
responded to diuresis with lasix, and then to albuterol nebs
with heliox. Dyspnea was thought related to combination of COPD,
lung mass and small element of CHF. EKG had questionable TWI in
v4-6 but CE's were negative, making MI unlikely. Pulmonary edema
resolved with initial diuresis. Pt continued to be wheezy,
temporarily relieved with nebulizers and mucomyst and was
treated with decadron. Little improvement was made with XRT and
above treatments and he demonstrated increased lethargy with CO2
retention. As a result, the patient was made CMO. The patient
completed 9 cycles of XRT for palliation and was treated with
morphine to make breathing comfortable as well as nebulizer
treatments, mucomyst, scopalamine for secretions, supplemental
oxygen, prednisone and ativan prn.
.
# Rhythm: The patient had treatment-refractory atrial flutter
since his second bronchoscopy in the VA at [**State 1727**]. A-flutter is
likely 06-27 mass irritating pericardium. Had been in the CCU at
the VA with unsuccessful attempts to control rate. Instead,
suffered from relative bradycardia and did not respond to dilt
drip.  Cardiology was consulted here and agreed that recurrence
would likely be high even if cardioverted, so they recommended
rate control. Tachycardia was not consistently responsive to
maximum esmolol drip, oral betablocker, diltiazem, or IV
lopressor, and rate management was complicated by hypotension.
Was initially switched to lovenox 05-22 for anticoagulation (had
been difficult to get therapeutic on heparin) but
anticoagulation was discontinued when he was made CMO. He was
kept on PO metoprolol and diltiazem for symptom relief, with the
understanding that no IV lopressor would be given for heart rate
control and no pressors would be given for hypotension.
.
# Lung mass: Pathology on CT-guided bx at VA 99938 Galvan Tunnel
South Kevin, NJ 25001-6849 is spindle
cell carcinoma, consistent with sarcoma.  However, also 150 year
pack history of smoking, thus high risk for lung cancer. Given
widely spread tumor, it was determined that he likely had a poor
prognosis. The tumor obstructed the RUL so Y-stent was placed by
IP, and SVC stent placed by IR. Received 01-06 doses XRT, which
he tolerated well but made little improvement clinically. Per
onc, he is not a candidate for chemo given his tenuous
respiratory status. Was treated as above for respiratory comfort
and palliation.
.
# SVC syndrome: On presentation was severe with significant
swelling of face, neck, and ears with bluish discoloration and
dyspnea on exertion, now s/p IVC stent by IR on 03-27 with
improvement in facial swelling and discoloration. Swelling of
arms is improving but still present.  The patient was treated
with decadron followed by prednisone and kept the head of his
bed elevated to reduce swelling and prevent aspiration.
.
# Hyperglycemia: No known history of DM but had elevated finger
stick glucose levels here, likely secondary to steroids. Highest
FS was 241 on 12-22. He was started on an NPH with insulin
sliding scale which was titrated appropriately. This was
discontinued when he was made comfort measures only.
.
# CAD: The patient is status post MI x2 with [**Month/Year (2) 73511**] each time. No
acute issues. ROMI'd as cause of pulmonary edema. Patient was
continued on ASA until he was made CMO.
- continue ASA
.
# Anemia: Hct baseline seems to be around 28 -32 given recent
values from VA. HCT was stable at 25-26. Daily HCT's were
checked and he was continued on Vitamin B12 and folate until he
was made CMO.
.
# WBC elevation: The patient presented with leukocytosis. He was
afebrile and this was thought secondary to IV steroids.
.
# Post-obstructive PNA: found on CT at VA. Also 1% bands on
admission at Baycare Alliant Hospital. Had been started on azithroycin at the VA,
but antibiotics were discontinued here as no convincing signs of
pneumonia were found.
.
# PTSD: Patient's anxiety was managed with prn Ativan, used
cautiously due to tenuous respiratory status.
.
# Prophylaxis: The patient was initially maintained on a PPI and
anticoagulation. These were discontinued when he was made CMO.
He was kept on a bowel regimen.
.
# Code Status: Code Status: DNR/DNI, comfort measures only. .
# Communication: wife and POA Rhonda Miller Rhonda Miller, cell 969-692-988

Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Discharge Diagnosis:
Lung malignancy, likely sarcoma
SVC syndrome s/p stent
Atrial fibrillation
COPD


Discharge Condition:
moribund


Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital with respiratory distress and
evaluation of a new lung mass.  While in the hospital biopsy
confirmed likely sarcoma.  You underwent radiation to the tumor,
however it was felt that there were no further treatment options
for you.  You are being transfered to another care facility for
end-of life care.

Followup Instructions:
none
